# Preface {.unnumbered}

This book teaches Git and GitHub to students and researchers in
economics, finance, accounting, and the social sciences. The
organizing principle is reproducibility: the ability for someone
else (including future you) to rerun your work and get the same
numbers. Git is the single most useful tool for getting there, and
it is increasingly a hard requirement. Journal data editors now
check that your replication package actually runs.

This is not a generic Git tutorial. Every example is built around
one small empirical project, a difference-in-differences study of a
minimum-wage change, and every command is run for real so the
output you see is the output the commands actually produced.

## Who this is for

Readers range from a first-year undergraduate who has never opened a
terminal to a PhD student assembling a replication package. No
computer-science background is assumed.

## How sections are tiered

Technical sections are tagged so you can read at your level:

::: {.tier-beginner}
### Read this if you are new

BEGINNER material is fully explained and copy-pasteable. If you type
exactly what you see, it will work.
:::

::: {.tier-advanced}
### Read this once the basics click

ADVANCED material assumes you are comfortable with the daily loop
and want the powerful, sharper tools.
:::

## The three callouts

Three callout boxes recur throughout the book.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
What commonly breaks, and the mistake that causes it.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
The exact commands to get out of trouble.
:::

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Why this step matters for someone else being able to rerun your work.
:::

## What Git does and does not do

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Git versions text: code, scripts, notes, small tables. Git does
**not** reproduce your data or your software environment. A repo
that contains only code is not a reproducible project. Chapters 6
and 8 are about closing that gap, and they matter as much as the
Git mechanics.
:::

## Conventions

Commands appear in blocks with their real output, followed by one
sentence on what changed and why. Terminal blocks are wrapped to
about 64 characters so they read cleanly in print and on the web.
We use modern Git throughout: the default branch is `main`, we use
`git switch` and `git restore` rather than `git checkout` for
everyday work, and we use `git filter-repo` (never BFG or
`filter-branch`) when history must be rewritten.
